domains.summary: The largest domain on the PTCE 2026. Focus on brand/generic pairs, drug classifications, common indications, insulin types, storage, and look-alike/sound-alike (LASA) drugs.

sections.title: Top Drug Classes & Suffixes
items: ACE inhibitors (-pril): lisinopril, enalapril, ramipril — hypertension, heart failure. Watch for dry cough, hyperkalemia.
ARBs (-sartan): losartan, valsartan — alternative to ACE inhibitors; less cough.
Beta blockers (-olol): metoprolol, atenolol, carvedilol — hypertension, angina, heart failure.
Calcium channel blockers: amlodipine (-dipine), diltiazem, verapamil — hypertension.
Statins (-statin): atorvastatin, simvastatin, rosuvastatin — lower LDL cholesterol.
Proton pump inhibitors (-prazole): omeprazole, pantoprazole, esomeprazole — GERD, ulcers.
H2 blockers (-tidine): famotidine — heartburn.
SSRIs: sertraline, fluoxetine, escitalopram — depression, anxiety.
Benzodiazepines (-pam/-lam): lorazepam, alprazolam — anxiety (C-IV controlled).

sections.title: Common Brand / Generic Pairs
items: Lipitor = atorvastatin
Prinivil/Zestril = lisinopril
Glucophage = metformin
Synthroid = levothyroxine
Prilosec = omeprazole
Norvasc = amlodipine
Coumadin = warfarin
Lasix = furosemide
Zoloft = sertraline
Neurontin = gabapentin
Ventolin/ProAir = albuterol
Zithromax = azithromycin

sections.title: Insulin Types
items: Rapid-acting: lispro (Humalog), aspart (NovoLog), glulisine (Apidra) — onset ~15 min.
Short-acting: regular (Humulin R, Novolin R) — onset ~30 min.
Intermediate: NPH (Humulin N, Novolin N).
Long-acting (basal): glargine (Lantus/Basaglar), detemir (Levemir), degludec (Tresiba).
Storage: unopened refrigerated 36–46°F; do NOT freeze; opened may be kept at room temp for a limited time.

sections.title: Common OTC Active Ingredients
items: Tylenol = acetaminophen (max 4,000 mg/day; hepatotoxic in overdose).
Advil/Motrin = ibuprofen (NSAID; take with food).
Aleve = naproxen (NSAID).
Claritin = loratadine; Zyrtec = cetirizine; Allegra = fexofenadine (2nd-gen antihistamines).
Benadryl = diphenhydramine (1st-gen; sedating).

sections.title: High-Alert & LASA Drugs
items: High-alert: insulin, anticoagulants (heparin, warfarin), opioids, concentrated electrolytes (KCl), chemotherapy.
LASA examples: hydrOXYzine vs hydrALAZINE, predniSONE vs prednisoLONE, DOPamine vs DOBUTamine — use tall-man lettering.

domains.summary: Patient Safety and Quality Assurance. Covers error prevention, high-alert meds, ISMP error-prone abbreviations, NDC format, hygiene, and reporting.

sections.title: Error Prevention Basics
items: Five rights: right patient, right drug, right dose, right route, right time.
Barcode scanning verifies the correct product against the order.
Independent double-checks for high-alert meds (e.g., insulin, heparin).
Medication reconciliation compares a patient's meds to new orders to prevent duplication/omission.

sections.title: ISMP Error-Prone Abbreviations (Avoid)
items: 'U' or 'u' → write 'units' (can look like 0 or 4).
'IU' → write 'international units'.
'QD'/'QOD' → write 'daily'/'every other day'.
Trailing zero (1.0 mg) → write 1 mg.
No leading zero (.5 mg) → write 0.5 mg.
'MS/MSO4/MgSO4' → spell out morphine sulfate or magnesium sulfate.

sections.title: NDC Format
items: National Drug Code = unique 10- or 11-digit, 3-segment number.
Segment 1 = labeler/manufacturer; Segment 2 = product (strength, dosage form); Segment 3 = package size.
Common formats: 5-4-2, 5-3-2, 4-4-2 (may be padded to 11 digits with a leading zero).

sections.title: Quality Assurance & Reporting
items: REMS: Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategy (e.g., iPLEDGE for isotretinoin, clozapine REMS).
Report errors/near-misses to improve systems (non-punitive culture).
MedWatch: FDA program for reporting adverse events.
USP <795> non-sterile and <797> sterile compounding standards; <800> hazardous drugs.

domains.summary: Order Entry and Processing. Sig code interpretation, pharmacy calculations, days supply, conversions, and prescription intake.

sections.title: Common Sig Codes
items: PO = by mouth; PR = rectally; SL = sublingual; IV/IM/SC = injection routes.
BID = twice daily; TID = 3x daily; QID = 4x daily; QHS = at bedtime; QAM = every morning.
PRN = as needed; ac = before meals; pc = after meals; q6h = every 6 hours.
gtt = drop; OD = right eye; OS = left eye; OU = both eyes; AD/AS/AU = right/left/both ears.
ii tab = 2 tablets; i cap = 1 capsule.

sections.title: Key Conversions
items: 1 tsp = 5 mL; 1 tbsp = 15 mL; 1 fl oz = 30 mL.
1 grain (gr) ≈ 65 mg; 1 oz = 28.4 g.
1 gallon = 3,785 mL; 1 pint = 473 mL.
Temperature: °C = (°F − 32) × 5/9.

sections.title: Core Calculations
items: Days supply = total quantity ÷ amount used per day.
Percent strength: X% = X grams per 100 mL (or 100 g).
IV flow rate (mL/hr) = total volume ÷ total time (hr).
Drops/min = (volume × drop factor) ÷ time in minutes.
Alligation: use for mixing two strengths to make an intermediate strength (parts method).

sections.title: Prescription Intake
items: Verify patient identifiers: full name and date of birth.
Confirm drug, strength, dosage form, quantity, directions, prescriber, and DEA (if controlled).
Check insurance/DUR and allergies before adjudication.
Technicians enter data; the pharmacist performs the final clinical verification.

domains.summary: Federal Requirements. DEA controlled substance schedules, DEA forms, refill rules, key laws (CSA, HIPAA, OBRA-90, PPPA, CMEA), and DEA number verification.

sections.title: DEA Controlled Substance Schedules
items: C-I: no accepted medical use, high abuse (heroin, LSD, marijuana federally).
C-II: high abuse, accepted use — NO refills (oxycodone, hydrocodone, morphine, Adderall, fentanyl).
C-III: moderate abuse (Tylenol #3, testosterone) — up to 5 refills / 6 months.
C-IV: lower abuse (alprazolam, lorazepam, tramadol, zolpidem) — up to 5 refills / 6 months.
C-V: lowest abuse (cough syrups with codeine, pregabalin) — up to 5 refills / 6 months.

sections.title: DEA Forms
items: Form 222: order/transfer Schedule II substances (electronic = CSOS).
Form 106: report theft or significant loss of controlled substances.
Form 41: record destruction/disposal of controlled substances.
Form 224: pharmacy application for DEA registration.

sections.title: Key Federal Laws
items: Controlled Substances Act (1970): created the 5-schedule system.
HIPAA (1996): protects patient PHI (privacy & security).
OBRA-90: offer to counsel + prospective DUR.
Poison Prevention Packaging Act (1970): child-resistant containers.
CMEA (2005): pseudoephedrine limits 3.6 g/day, 9 g/30 days; logbook + ID.
FDCA (1938) & Durham-Humphrey Amendment (1951): Rx vs OTC classification.
Biennial (every 2 years) controlled substance inventory required.

sections.title: DEA Number Verification
items: Format: 2 letters + 7 digits. First letter = registrant type; second letter = registrant's last-name initial.
Check digit math: (1st+3rd+5th digits) + 2×(2nd+4th+6th digits) = sum; the last digit of the sum must equal the 7th (check) digit.
Example: AB1234563 → (1+3+5)=9; (2+4+6)=12 ×2 =24; 9+24=33; last digit 3 = check digit 3. Valid.
